definition: The Spiritual Flood — the outpouring of divine grace that Shaykh Ibrāhīm Niasse identified as the defining spiritual event of the age, predicted in the traditions of the Tijānī order. The term denotes both the historical phenomenon of Niasse's mass spiritual education movement and the metaphysical reality of divine emanation from which it draws its name.

Spiritual education and formation — the guided process by which a murshid (spiritual guide) leads a disciple toward the realization of tawḥīd. In Niasse's practice, tarbiya was a rapid and intensive method distinct from the slower methods of earlier Sufi masters.

Divine unity — the foundational Islamic doctrine of God's oneness. In Niasse's tafsīr, tawḥīd operates at multiple registers: the affirmation of God's existence (tawḥīd al-dhāt), His attributes (tawḥīd al-ṣifāt), and His acts (tawḥīd al-afʿāl). The deeper register — realised tawḥīd — is the experiential extinction of the self in the awareness of divine unity.

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: فيفنى العبد ويفنى الوجود ويحصل التوحيد الحقيقي والحياة الأبدية
niasse_evidence.translation_note: The servant becomes annihilated, existence becomes annihilated, and the real tawḥīd and eternal life are attained

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: تجد فيه المواعظ والتذكير والخبر والتوحيد
niasse_evidence.translation_note: You find in it exhortation, reminder, report, and tawḥīd

definition: Annihilation — the Sufi technical term for the dissolution of the ego-self in the awareness of God. Niasse treats fanāʾ not as a permanent obliteration but as a station (maqām) on the way to baqāʾ (subsistence in God), following the classical Sufi understanding.

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: فمن بذل ماله لله وجد الفناء في الأفعال، يعلم أن لا فاعل في الوجود إلا الله
niasse_evidence.translation_note: Whoever gives his wealth for God finds annihilation in the acts — knowing there is no agent in existence but God

apparatus_evidence.arabic_quote: بذكر القلب يجد الفناء في الصفات، وبذكر الروح يجد الفناء الكلي
apparatus_evidence.note: Compiler on three degrees of fanāʾ: in acts (afʿāl), attributes (ṣifāt), and totality (kullī)

definition: Subsistence — the station following fanāʾ (annihilation), in which the mystic continues to exist but in a transformed state: the self subsists in and through God. The pair fanāʾ/baqāʾ is foundational to Niasse's anthropology.

The Perfect Human — a central concept in Ibn ʿArabī's metaphysics, taken up by Niasse to describe the fully realised human being who serves as the locus of divine self-disclosure. Niasse identifies this figure with the Prophet Muḥammad as the universal archetype and with the awliyāʾ (saints) as its partial manifestations.

Divine self-disclosure — the act by which God makes Himself known through the cosmos, the Quran, and the human heart. Niasse follows Ibn ʿArabī in understanding creation as a continuous unfolding of divine self-disclosure.

Sainthood / divine friendship — the station of proximity to God attained by the awliyāʾ (saints). Niasse treats walāya as the inner dimension of prophecy, continuing after the seal of prophethood in the lineage of spiritual heirs.

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: خاتما للولاية المحمدية تشريفا لهذه الأمة، فلا ولاية بعد نزول عيسى
niasse_evidence.translation_note: Sealing the Muhammadan sainthood as an honour to this community; no walāya after the descent of Jesus

apparatus_evidence.arabic_quote: أولياء اللّٰه بالولاية الصغرى... وأولياء اللّٰه بالولاية الكبرى
apparatus_evidence.note: Compiler distinguishing walāya ṣughrā (minor sainthood) from walāya kubrā (major sainthood)

definition: The isthmus — a term with both cosmological and eschatological meanings in Niasse's tafsīr. Cosmologically, it denotes the intermediary reality between two orders of being (e.g., between the divine and creaturely realms). Eschatologically, it refers to the intermediary state between death and resurrection.

Spiritual station — a stable, achieved level of spiritual realisation, as distinct from ḥāl (spiritual state), which is transient. Niasse's tafsīr maps the Quranic narrative onto a sequence of spiritual stations traversed on the path to God.

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: لكل مقام مقال... مقام التذكر
niasse_evidence.translation_note: For every maqām there is a corresponding utterance... the station of recollection (tatadhakkur)

definition: Inimitability — the doctrine that the Quran cannot be matched or replicated by human composition, establishing its miraculous nature. Niasse engages this doctrine at length in his discussion of the opening suras, situating it within the broader tradition of Quranic sciences.

The disjointed letters — the letter-combinations that open twenty-nine Quranic suras (Alif Lām Mīm, Yā Sīn, etc.). Niasse discusses multiple interpretive traditions — grammatical, mystical, and prophetic — and gives his own reading, which tends toward the Sufi understanding of these letters as keys to divine secrets.
[PENDING — awaiting AK's definition drawing on Mishri]

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: المعرفة روح روح الروح، المعرفة روح العقل، والعقل روح الجسد
niasse_evidence.translation_note: Maʿrifa is the spirit of the spirit of the spirit; maʿrifa is the spirit of the intellect, and the intellect is the spirit of the body

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: والجنة المعنوية هي المعرفة بالله تبارك وتعالى
niasse_evidence.translation_note: The inward Garden is maʿrifa of God

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: الرؤية التي هي معرفة الله، معرفة اللّٰه أولا بالإيمان
niasse_evidence.translation_note: The vision that is maʿrifa of God — maʿrifa of God first through faith

apparatus_evidence.arabic_quote: ولاية الكبرى من عرف اللّٰه معرفة فوق الإيمان وفوق الإيقان بل معرفة المشاهدة والعيان
apparatus_evidence.note: Compiler citing Niasse's distinction between levels of maʿrifa

definition: [PENDING — awaiting AK's definition drawing on Mishri]

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: مشرب السر الأنس والهيبة
niasse_evidence.translation_note: The drinking-place of the sirr is intimacy (uns) and awe (hayba)

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: والسر يفنى في ذات اللّٰه تبارك وتعالى
niasse_evidence.translation_note: The sirr becomes annihilated in the Essence of God

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: ولابد من الذكر باللسان أولا ثم بالقلب ثم بالروح والسر حتى يصل إلى المطلوب
niasse_evidence.translation_note: Dhikr must proceed: tongue, then heart (qalb), then spirit (rūḥ), then sirr — until the sought arrives

apparatus_evidence.arabic_quote: لباس النفس ولباس القلب ولباس الروح ولباس السر
apparatus_evidence.note: Compiler on the four vestments of spiritual faculties: nafs, qalb, rūḥ, sirr

definition: [PENDING — awaiting AK's definition drawing on Mishri]

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: المعرفة روح العقل، والعقل روح الجسد
niasse_evidence.translation_note: Maʿrifa is the spirit of the intellect; the intellect is the spirit of the body

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: الروح هو السلطان والعقل هو الوزير والقلب هو المفتي
niasse_evidence.translation_note: The rūḥ is the sovereign, the intellect is the vizier, the heart is the jurist

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: مشرب الروح مشاهدة الجمال والجلال
niasse_evidence.translation_note: The drinking-place of the rūḥ is the vision of Beauty (jamāl) and Majesty (jalāl)

apparatus_evidence.arabic_quote: ولباس الروح التقوى ولكن تقوى يمنعه من الالتفات إلى غير اللّٰه
apparatus_evidence.note: Compiler on the vestment of the rūḥ: taqwā that prevents turning toward other than God

definition: [PENDING — awaiting AK's definition drawing on Mishri]

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: لما أراد إظهار وجود غير وجوده تجلى في الحقيقة المحمدية فهي أول تجلٍ بعد الغيب المحض
niasse_evidence.translation_note: When God willed to manifest an existence other than His own, He disclosed Himself in the Muhammadan Reality — it is the first tajallī after sheer hiddenness

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: هذا الروح هو الحقيقة المحمدية
niasse_evidence.translation_note: This spirit is the Muhammadan Reality

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: اللّٰه تبارك وتعالى أول ما أوجد درة بيضاء وهي الحقيقة المحمدية
niasse_evidence.translation_note: The first thing God brought into existence was a white pearl — that is the Muhammadan Reality

apparatus_evidence.arabic_quote: هذا الروح هو الذي به ضل النصارى
apparatus_evidence.note: Compiler contextualising the Muhammadan Reality against Christian deviation

definition: [PENDING — awaiting AK's definition drawing on Mishri]

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: قصدوا الحضرة، إذا صدقوا في أنّهم لا يقصدون غير الحضرة فهم لا مقصود عندهم إلا الله
niasse_evidence.translation_note: They aimed for the Divine Presence (ḥaḍra); if they were sincere in seeking nothing but the ḥaḍra, then they have no goal but God

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: إلى أن تجد الأنس والهيبة بسرادقات الحضرة
niasse_evidence.translation_note: Until you find intimacy and awe in the pavilions of the Divine Presence

apparatus_evidence.arabic_quote: إذا ضربنا هذه الأسماء في الحضرات الثلاث الحقائق الثلاث
apparatus_evidence.note: Compiler on the three ḥaḍarāt — the three divine presences

definition: [PENDING — awaiting AK's definition drawing on Mishri]

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: بدعوى الوصول إلى اللّٰه تبارك وتعالى بدون السلوك
niasse_evidence.translation_note: Claiming to reach God without sulūk [Niasse dismisses such claims as ẓulm]

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: سلوك غير الأنبياء من النفس الأمارة بالسوء إلى أن تصل إلى الكمال، وسلوك الأنبياء من النفس المطمئنة
niasse_evidence.translation_note: The sulūk of non-prophets proceeds from the commanding soul (nafs ammāra) toward perfection; the sulūk of prophets begins from the tranquil soul (nafs muṭmaʾinna)

apparatus_evidence.arabic_quote: الإخوان في السلوك المريدون الصادقون ينتفعون بعضهم من بعض
apparatus_evidence.note: Compiler: brothers in sulūk — sincere murīdūn benefit one another

definition: [PENDING — awaiting AK's definition drawing on Mishri]

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: فاضت عليها فيضة أخرى من رحمة اللّٰه تبارك وتعالى
niasse_evidence.translation_note: Another outpouring (fayḍa) of divine mercy flooded over it [the soul]

apparatus_evidence.note: Compiler citing Ibn ʿArabī on fayḍ via Fayḍ al-Qadīr
